tier_prediction.mystic_verdict: Bronze stirs. Mrs Airma offers her encouragement.
tier_prediction.synthesis: A memorable scientific reframe and a real product give it medal credibility, but the impact case is intent-survey heavy with no sales yet and largely Swedish earned coverage, which caps the jury read around Bronze.

medal_prediction.synthesis: A memorable scientific reframe and a real product give it medal credibility, but the impact case is intent-survey heavy with no sales yet and largely Swedish earned coverage, which caps the jury read around Bronze.

extracted.tagline: You are not "Picky" You are a SUPERTASTER. And this is your Supercube
extracted.context: Knorr's mission is to make healthy vegetables irresistible, but some people genuinely hate vegetables.
extracted.insight: 1 in 4 people are born with a "supertaster" gene (TAS2R38) that makes vegetables like broccoli, kale, spinach and cabbage taste extremely bitter — they aren't picky, they're biologically wired differently.
extracted.idea: The Supercube — the first stock cube developed to reduce bitter flavours, and the first food product made specifically for supertasters, helping a quarter of the population enjoy a veggie-packed diet.
execution: Film showing supertasters (child, adult) reacting to bitter vegetables
Knorr Superkub product packaging (stock cube box)
Reframing messaging cards: 'You are not picky / difficult / awkward — you are a Supertaster'
Taste-test reaction content ('Oh my god, it was delicious!')
Earned media / news and daytime TV coverage
metrics: 70% of consumers would buy the Supercube

inferred.one_liner: The first stock cube for supertasters.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed picky eaters as biologically distinct 'supertasters', destigmatising a quarter of the population and giving Knorr a new untapped product category set for retail launch in 2025.

visual.style_description: Cinematic, warm-toned lifestyle photography paired with bold sans-serif headline typography and a radiating sunburst graphic behind the hero product. Case-study layout is clean and editorial with a filmstrip of supporting stills.

idea.rationale: Reframing 'picky eaters' as biologically distinct 'supertasters' via the TAS2R38 gene is a fresh, identity-relabeling hook with strong mnemonic potential, anchored to a tangible new product (the Supercube). It's more strategically rooted than a one-gag stunt like Heatsuit but less culturally explosive than Ketchup Fraud's category-defining frame.

strategy.rationale: Knorr's stated mission is making vegetables irresistible, and a product engineered to mask bitterness for 25% of the population is tightly coherent with that mission. The TAS2R38 gene is a real, verifiable scientific insight, and the destigmatising angle creates earned-media fuel. Strategy is on-brand and insight-led, comparable to Vaseline Transition in mission-fit.

execution.rationale: Cinematic film, neon identity, reframing cards and a real packaged product (Superkub) show coherent craft across owned/earned layers, plus 30,000 gene tests as a participatory mechanic. Press list is decent (Daily Mail, regional Swedish outlets) but skews mid-tier and Sweden-centric, not a global earned wave.

impact.rationale: 144M reach and 30,000 gene tests are concrete, but '70% would buy' and '85% see Knorr as more innovative' are survey intent metrics, not sales — and the product only reaches shops in 2025, so no real business outcome yet. Mirrors the Vaseline/Heatsuit pattern of percentages without absolute commercial base.

why_it_won.verdict: A genuinely original scientific reframe tied to a real product launch, but the impact case rests on intent surveys and reach rather than sales — placing it in Bronze territory alongside Vaseline Transition and Heatsuit, with upside to Silver if jury rewards the category-creating product.

references.one_liner: Refilling a Heinz bottle with another brand is fraud.
references.verdict: Lands Gold because a real, observable consumer suspicion is converted into a brand-owned crime ('Ketchup Fraud') that defends the exact equity Heinz sells on — proven by an 8% sales lift, not just a press wall.

Bronze because the product is genuinely category-first and on-mission for Vaseline, but every headline impact figure is a percentage without a stated base and the campaign architecture beyond launch is shallow.

references.one_liner: Snoop quits smoke. (Then reveals: smokeless fire pits.)
references.verdict: Bronze because one ambiguous post genuinely hijacked a global cultural moment, but the impact case is a stack of percentage-and-impression claims with no absolute base and the longer-tail commercial outcome did not match the headlines.

references.one_liner: A viral Taylor Swift photo becomes a Heinz product overnight.
references.verdict: Bronze because the real-time hijack delivered fast, broad earned coverage and a permanent Walmart SKU, but the case is reactive opportunism with a 320% lift whose absolute baseline (Kranch was a flop) makes the headline percentage flattering.

references.one_liner: The pizza heat bag becomes a wearable suit.
references.verdict: Bronze because the stunt does exactly what a UK tabloid PR brief asks for — visual, on-brand, sales-shifting — but it is a single-gag activation with an unbased 18% sales claim rather than a strategic idea.

notes: Network: agency belongs to IPG → no malus
